Generally, log-based anomaly detection can be performed using either point, collective, or contextual approaches. In the log-based anomaly detection literature, most of the existing studies propose either a contextual or collective approach, where the decision is taken by examining a sequence of log events. However, detecting abnormalities in log data can be performed using a point-based approach, where one single log record is the only feature needed. Therefore, several studies explored utilising a point-based approach to detect anomalous events on logs data, such as in operating systems , , drone devices , and distribution systems , . source_paragraph: several studies explored utilising a point-based approach to detect anomalous events on logs data, such as in operating systems , , drone devices , and distribution systems , . In the drone context, a log message contains a description of occurring events which are triggered by various components such as sensors, peripherals, and firmware. Analysing the log message means analysing events from all aspects of the drone, including sensors, components and features . Typically, existing log-based anomaly detection stud- ies classify log events into two categories: normal and anomaly . This is helpful in an online setting, where the number of detected abnormalities in a certain period of time is considerably small. However, in a digital forensic setting, where the

source_paragraph: classify log events into two categories: normal and anomaly . This is helpful in an online setting, where the number of detected abnormalities in a certain period of time is considerably small. However, in a digital forensic setting, where the detection is performed on all collected artefacts which might be large in size, the number of the abnormalities is likely to be large . Thus, analysing the detection result in a binary setting is impractical for the investigator. Moreover, out of all detected peculiarities, there might be several negligible ones with less severe impacts on the system or less likely to be related to an incident. Extending binary to multiclass anomaly detection can provide more detailed and contextual detection results

CLASS IMBALANCE PROBLEM (CIP) IN LOG-BASED ANOMALY DETECTION It is assured that in log-based anomaly detection, the number of anomalous samples is significantly less than the normal ones. Employing a supervised-based technique is prone to bias, as the model tends to learn from the majority samples. To overcome CIP, several studies proposed data-level and algorithm-level solutions . Moreover, in a certain case, there are no anomalous samples available. In this particular situation, a one-class approach can be used to construct a normal baseline model. source_paragraph: PREVIOUS WORKS Among the relevant published studies in the log-based anomaly detection space, sequence-based is one of the most commonly used approaches. Therefore, there are limited relevant references as this study employs a point-based approach. Below are the relevant baselines from previous research: 1) Pylogsentiment is the first study which employs sentiment analysis-based anomaly detection on operat- ing system logs using GRU and GloVe embedding. 2) SentiLog , similar to , use a two-layered BiLSTM and GloVe embedding model to perform anomaly detection on parallel file system logs. 3) TransSentLog is a further development of and uses a two-layered transformer encoder which used two attention heads and GloVe embedding along with integrated gradients to add explainability to the trained model.

In this section, challenges encountered during the study are disclosed, along with limitations and threats to validity. As drone forensics is an emerging topic, there are very limited public datasets available. The log messages used in this study are mainly acquired from DJI-made devices. There is yet to come a publicly available dataset of log messages from other drone manufacturers. Several publicly available datasets are mainly about sensor data and multimedia artefacts . While in this study, we solely depend on the human-readable messages generated by the drone during a flight. CONCLUSION AND FUTURE WORK In this paper, we demonstrate how to train a log-based anomaly detection model that can prioritise the prediction of higher severity anomalies on drone flight logs while increasing the prediction confidence score at the same time. Considering the nature of the dataset, where samples that belong to different severity levels share common features, a multitask vector label representation along with severity-oriented decoding is proposed. An extensive experiment proved that the proposed approach is better than the previous work baselines while being inferior to our baseline scenario based on the accuracy and F1

source_paragraph: I. INTRODUCTION I. INTRODUCTION The use of drones has become increasingly common in various applications, including aerial photography, surveying, and monitoring. However, drones can also be involved in incidents such as accidents, violations, or even crimes, making it necessary to investigate drone logs as part of forensic investigations. In a forensic investigation, drone logs are essential sources of information that can help investigators reconstruct the events leading up to an incident. Drone logs typically include information such as flight paths, altitude, speed, battery life, sensor data, and other telemetry information . Important analysis of drone logs involves the identification of the most critical pieces of information in the log data and the analysis of how they relate to the incident

source_paragraph: DATASET The DEAP dataset has 32 participants with a composition of 50% male and 50% female samples . The stimulus used is a video with a duration of 60 seconds. The results of the EEG recording have a duration of 63 seconds, of which 3 seconds are the baseline. One participant has 40 recordings of the EEG trials. The results of the EEG trial recordings have been mea- sured using the Self-Assessment Manikin (SAM). SAM has four emotional dimensions, namely valence, arousal, domi- nance, and liking, with ratings 0-9. For valence and arousal classes, emotion labels are divided into two categories: low and high. In a forensic setting, analysing a large number of log entries resulting from binary anomaly detection is considered inefficient and labour-intensive as the detection is performed on the acquired evidence at one time . Therefore, too many detected anomalies need to be detailed into a more fine- grained detection to help the investigator better prioritise the analysis objective. As a result, this study extends the binary anomaly detection to multi-class anomaly detection, as exemplified by another domain application such as plane flight data , image data , and internet of vehicle . source_paragraph: Existing anomaly detection modelling techniques can be conducted either in a point, contextual, or collective setting. Point anomaly detection characterises each log entry, whether an event is an abnormality or a normal event. For instance, if a user types the wrong password when trying to gain access to an operating system prompt, the generated log message can be considered as an anomaly , , . As for contextual anomaly, the detection involves analysing some contextually related attributes to determine whether a log entry is an anomaly. As an example, the flight path taken by a drone during a flight is recorded in longitude and latitude coordinates. These data can be used to detect an anomaly by finding a peculiar value

These data can be used to detect an anomaly by finding a peculiar value within the log records by contrasting the normal values to abnormal values using a certain threshold . Unique to the previous two approaches, collective anomaly establishes anomaly criteria based on a set of instances associated with each other, distinct from the overall data. Statistical anomaly detection is one of the common cases for this type of anomaly detection. source_paragraph: align with the anomaly severity context, where the distance between true and predicted labels matters. If the predicted label is lower than the true label, we define it False Positive (FP). Conversely, if the true label is lower than the predicted label, we define it False Negative (FN). In this context, having less FP is more important than having less FN, as the task objective is anomaly severity detection, where we aim to detect all abnormalities along with the severity level. Therefore, undetected abnormalities, or detecting higher severity as lower ones, indicate that the model fails to meet the task’s objectives. Despite the promising results, the model’s generality remains untested as the training dataset contains only log messages acquired from 82 drone models, mostly from DJI. This is one of the main challenges in drone forensics research, where human-readable flight log datasets from other drone models are unavailable. In the future, we plan to explore the flight logs of other drone models to train more general anomaly detection models. As for the method, multi- objective learning that employs multiple loss functions to train an anomaly detection model will be explored. This contribution aims to streamline the implementation of emotion recognition systems without compromising perfor- mance. The proposed research is based on a simplified deep learn- ing architecture and channel selection approach that leads towards closer and resource-constrained real-time implemen- tation. In addition, research for cross-subject validation is more likely to be implemented widely than dependent subject validation. Cross-subject validation has a better generaliza- tion model than dependent-subject validation. Next, we fine-tuned this model on our research dataset consisting of laparoscopic images to adapt the model to the specific characteristics of peritoneal carcinomatosis. This fine-tuning process ensures that the model not only recognizes general features from the COCO dataset but also specific features relevant to the task of detecting peritoneal carcinomatosis, thus enhancing the model's performance in detecting lesions with high accuracy. source_paragraph: can be fine-tuned into our dataset. V. CONCLUSION V. CONCLUSION This research demonstrates that the YOLOv8 model, specifically the YOLOv8m variant, provides excellent results in detecting Peritoneal Carcinomatosis (PC) from the used laparoscopic image dataset. By utilizing and fine-tuning the pre-trained YOLOv8 weights, this model shows superior performance with a mean Average Precision (mAP50) of 0.871, precision of 0.893, recall of 0.785, and an F1 score of 0.84. The predicted images also show that the performance of YOLOv8 with pre-trained weights is able to predict images well. All YOLOv8 scales are accurate in making predictions, with no errors in image prediction. In contrast, without using pre-trained weights, YOLOv8n and YOLOv8s still make errors in prediction, with some lesions being missed.
